perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The Parties irrevocably agree that the Courts of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The exclusive jurisdiction clause has been removed from the governing law section. This weakens the enforceability of the agreement as it relates to jurisdiction, creating ambiguity regarding where disputes must be settled. The next section will contain this clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit jurisdiction clause from the governing law section and placing it elsewhere (see below), the contract introduces ambiguity regarding where disputes must be settled. This could potentially allow parties to attempt to invoke diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S. Code § 1332 in federal courts outside of Delaware, arguing that the contract is unclear about the exclusive jurisdiction. The original clause was very clear about Delaware courts having exclusive jurisdiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that Delaware courts have exclusive jurisdiction. The changed text removes this exclusivity, potentially opening the door to other jurisdictions, as explained in the Law Explanation. This is supported by the Perturbation Explanation, which states that removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause weakens the agreement's enforceability. While the scraped snippets focus on diversity jurisdiction (28 U.S. Code § 1332), the core issue is that removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause creates ambiguity and allows for interpretations contrary to the original intent of having disputes settled solely in Delaware, creating a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction because it weakens enforceability by creating ambiguity.
